macro: Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's push for 'price stability' faces an early test: CME FedWatch still prices roughly 89% odds of a hold at the July 28-29 meeting, unchanged even as oil surged on a renewed US-Iran conflict. Preliminary July University of Michigan sentiment jumped 9.9% to 54.4, beating the 51.0 forecast, as the one-year inflation outlook eased to 4.2% from 4.6% — but June housing starts also surged 19% to a 1.427 million pace while permits slipped to a 10-month low, a mixed signal on the same day CENTCOM struck Iran for a seventh consecutive night and WTI rose more than 14% for the week. The date to watch this week: Friday's S&P Global flash PMI, the last broad economic read before the Fed's blackout period ahead of July 28-29.
btc_status: Bitcoin trades near $63,000, unable to reclaim its roughly $62,600 200-week moving average, which has flipped from support to resistance for the first time this cycle. The Fear & Greed Index reads 27 (Fear), little changed from a day earlier; the level to watch is a reclaim of the 50-day EMA near $65,600, with a break below the $58,115 monthly low reopening a path toward $55,000.
markets:
TOP OPPORTUNITIES
1. TRV — 71/100 | Q2 EPS of $10.04 crushed the $5.41 consensus as catastrophe losses nearly halved; shares jumped 8% to a 52-week high on heavy volume, trading above both the 50-day and 200-day averages.
2. MPC — 64/100 | Citi raised its price target to $303 from $257 as Hormuz-driven crude gains widen refining margins; shares hold above both key averages with RSI near 63.

AVOID TODAY
AMD — Fell as much as 7% on July 17, caught in a broader semiconductor rout that has erased $3.3 trillion in chip-sector value since June 22; Q4 revenue guidance of roughly $7.5 billion missed consensus, with gaming revenue down 69% year-over-year.
energy: WTI settled near $82.47 a barrel, up 4.46% on the day and roughly 14% higher for the week, while Brent pushed toward the mid-$80s as CENTCOM struck Iran for a seventh consecutive night. The EIA's most recent report (week ended July 10, released July 15) showed crude inventories fell 1.69 million barrels, a smaller draw than forecast, with the next release due July 22. The Strait of Hormuz remains the dominant supply risk, with roughly a fifth of global oil transiting the corridor amid the escalating conflict.
vix_and_dollar: The VIX closed at 18.77 on July 17, up 12.19% and near the top of the 15-20 'normal' band as the semiconductor selloff and Iran escalation both weighed on sentiment. The Dollar Index (DXY) held near 100.70, little changed after mixed housing and industrial data; a jumping VIX alongside a flat dollar suggests investors are hedging equity risk rather than fleeing to cash broadly.
mortgage_rate: The 30-year fixed mortgage averaged 6.55% in Freddie Mac's survey for the week of July 16, 2026, unchanged since the next PMMS reading isn't due until July 23. Existing home sales fell 2.4% in June to a 4.09 million pace even as the Housing Affordability Index improved to 102.3, a reminder that thin inventory, not just rates, is holding back transactions.
personal_finance_deep_dive: The New York Fed's Q1 2026 Household Debt and Credit Report shows student loan delinquency climbed to 10.3% of balances 90-plus days past due, up from 9.6% the prior quarter and the highest share since 2020, with roughly 2.6 million borrowers defaulting in the quarter alone. The surge follows the end of pandemic-era forbearance protections and is landing on household budgets already stretched by record credit card balances and a savings rate well below its historical average. Borrowers who didn't complete a degree are consistently the most exposed, since they carry loan balances without the earnings bump a diploma is meant to provide. It adds a fourth stress point — after credit cards, auto loans, and now student debt — to a household balance sheet with progressively less room to absorb a new shock.
wealth_building_deep_dive: U.S.-listed ETFs pulled in a record $196 billion in June alone, State Street Investment Management says, capping a record $560 billion quarter and putting the industry on pace for $2.3 trillion of inflows this year, well above 2025's record pace. The flows kept coming even as headline volatility jumped this week, a sign the dominant investor behavior right now is scheduled buying through funds rather than reacting to daily headlines. The mechanism behind both the institutional and individual version of this is identical: routing money into a diversified, low-cost fund on a fixed schedule rather than trying to time entries around news like this week's chip selloff. A reader can apply this literally this week by confirming an automatic investment is still set to execute on payday, regardless of what the VIX or any single sector does in the meantime.
notable: Netflix shares hit a fresh 52-week low of $65.65 on July 17 after Q3 revenue guidance of $12.86 billion missed the roughly $13 billion consensus, overshadowing an in-line Q2 print (Fortune; TradingKey, Jul 17, 2026).

Global semiconductor stocks have shed $3.3 trillion in market value since June 22, the worst weekly rout for chipmakers since the April 2025 tariff meltdown, after China's Moonshot unveiled its open-weight Kimi K3 model (Bloomberg, Jul 17, 2026).

Berkshire Hathaway rose more than 1% on July 17 as investors rotated into defensive value names — Travelers, Textron, and Jefferies Financial all gained 3%-8% in the same session (market reports, Jul 17, 2026).
